title: Upgrade, CONNECT, Tunnels, WebSocket Boundaries, and Safe Transitions · Runtime Contract: Transition Rejection Recovery
summary: A client sends new-protocol bytes before acceptance, a server parses tunneled data as another HTTP request, buffered bytes disappear during handoff, and rejected transitions desynchronize the connection. Planned artifact: Add and verify the transition rejection recovery increment in confirmed protocol-transition and buffered-byte handoff state machine. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Continue HTTP/1.1 safely after a rejected transition only when buffered bytes, framing, peer behavior, and parser state remain unambiguous.
Apply RFC 9931 by forbidding unsafe optimistic new-protocol bytes in HTTP/1.1 until transition acceptance is known.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: Predict Transition Rejection Recovery · Theory Transition Rejection Recovery
steps.instruction:
theory case — A theory protocol team handles case 6290 in a field-normalizing cache with an independent origin parser. Build deterministic Go evidence to wait for confirmed Upgrade or CONNECT acceptance before transferring parser ownership; preserve this operating constraint: diagnostics must omit credentials and attacker-controlled metric labels; then replace one field with repeated non-combinable lines and preserve their order. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Continue HTTP/1.1 safely after a rejected transition only when buffered bytes, framing, peer behavior, and parser state remain unambiguous.

Before reading, predict which action preserves the Transition Rejection Recovery contract for the supplied data.

steps.title: Defend the Transition Rejection Recovery model · Theory Transition Rejection Recovery
steps.instruction:
Write the theory evidence note for Transition Rejection Recovery: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A theory protocol team handles case 6296 in a incident replay lab retaining minimized request-smuggling corpora. Build deterministic Go evidence to wait for confirmed Upgrade or CONNECT acceptance before transferring parser ownership; preserve this operating constraint: diagnostics must omit credentials and attacker-controlled metric labels; then inject Transfer-Encoding plus Content-Length and close before any downstream forwarding. Browser code models bounded byte and state decisions only. Sockets, listeners, live TLS, proxies, packet capture, fuzz campaigns, races, load, HTTP/2, HTTP/3, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Continue HTTP/1.1 safely after a rejected transition only when buffered bytes, framing, peer behavior, and parser state remain unambiguous.
